summary: Powell Smythe served in the South Carolina House of Representatives in 1868, representing Clarendon County during Reconstruction. He was part of a cohort of newly enfranchised Black legislators who participated in reshaping state governance after the Civil War, and his photograph appears in a montage of Radical Republican legislators held by the Smithsonian.

historicalContext: During Reconstruction, formerly enslaved men gained the right to vote and hold office, leading to unprecedented Black political representation in Southern legislatures. In South Carolina, where Black people constituted a majority of the population, this resulted in significant legislative influence, including the 1868 constitutional convention and new laws promoting civil rights and public education.

summary: Samuel B. Thompson served as a Black legislator from Richland County in the South Carolina House of Representatives from 1868 to 1874 and participated in the 1865 Constitutional Convention, during a brief era of Black political inclusion after emancipation. He also served as a justice of the peace, contributing to local governance during Reconstruction.

claims.object: member of the South Carolina House of Representatives
justice of the peace for eight years

claims.citationLabel: SC Department of Archives and History - Randolph Cemetery gravemarker

historicalContext: Thompson’s election followed the enfranchisement of Black men under the Reconstruction Acts of 1867, which enabled formerly enslaved people to vote and hold office. His service occurred during a transformative period when Black legislators in South Carolina advanced public education and civil rights legislation, despite rising white resistance and the eventual rollback of gains after 1877.

summary: John B. Wright, a free Black man before the Civil War, served in the South Carolina House of Representatives from 1868 to 1872 during Reconstruction. A literate tailor and civic leader, he was president of the Colored Young Men's Christian Association and depicted in a contemporary photograph of Radical Republican legislators, reflecting his role in early Black political empowerment.

claims.object: Black and free before the Civil War
1868 photograph of Radical Republican South Carolina legislators
literate Black man in postbellum South Carolina

historicalContext: Wright’s election followed the enfranchisement of Black men under the Reconstruction Acts of 1867, which allowed formerly enslaved and free Black men to hold office in newly restructured Southern governments. His service in a biracial legislature challenged prewar racial hierarchies and exemplified the brief window of Black political participation before Jim Crow laws dismantled these gains.

summary: Harry E. Hayne, born free in Charleston, served as South Carolina’s Secretary of State (1872–1877) and became the first Black student at the University of South Carolina’s medical school in 1873. A Republican and Union Army veteran, he was a delegate to the 1868 Constitutional Convention and led a Freedmen’s Bureau school.

historicalContext: Hayne’s political and educational breakthroughs occurred during Reconstruction, when federal enforcement enabled Black suffrage and officeholding in the South. His enrollment at the University of South Carolina tested the 1868 state constitution’s integration mandate, directly challenging Jim Crow’s precursors. The university’s subsequent closure and resegregation in 1877 exemplify the violent rollback of Black civil rights after federal withdrawal.
